qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: PHP Object Injection via unsafe unserialize() on ghost event_access_hidden POST parameter
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding concerns authenticated event-editor POST handling of event_access_hidden reaching unserialize() in my-calendar-event-editor.php. The advisory concerns the unauthenticated mcjs_action AJAX loadupcoming path where args is parsed and a site value is passed to switch_to_blog(), causing multisite IDOR information disclosure or single-site DoS. The vulnerable entrypoint, function, sink, authorization model, and root-cause mechanism are different.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described vulnerable code or equivalent root cause. The only qualifying finding is PHP object injection via unsafe unserialize() of event_access_hidden in the authenticated event editor, while the advisory is an unauthenticated IDOR/DoS through mc_ajax_mcjs_action, parse_str() of args, and unchecked switch_to_blog() on the site argument.
evidence: Advisory GHSA-2mvx-f5qm-v2ch describes add_action('wp_ajax_nopriv_mcjs_action', 'mc_ajax_mcjs_action'), behavior=loadupcoming, parse_str() over the args request parameter, and my_calendar_upcoming_events() calling switch_to_blog($args['site']) without authorization.

Finding php_object_injection_unserialize reports POST /wp-admin/admin.php?page=my-calendar, mc_admin_save() -> mc_insert_event() -> mc_check_data(), and unserialize($post['event_access_hidden']) in my-calendar-event-editor.php.

The finding requires an authenticated user with mc_add_events capability and targets PHP object injection; the advisory requires no authentication and targets cross-site event disclosure through multisite blog switching or single-site PHP worker failure.
